source_concept.label: Describe different types of supply chain management planning.

relationships.correct_description: Static and dynamic planning are approaches applied within these planning levels.
relationships.explanation: The three levels of supply chain planning -- strategic, tactical, and operational -- each can employ static or dynamic planning approaches. Static planning uses fixed plans regenerated periodically, while dynamic planning updates continuously as conditions change. Understanding which approach fits each planning level helps organizations balance stability with responsiveness.

sources.title: Mastering the Three Levels of Supply Chain Management
Supply Chain Planning 101: From Basics to Best Practices
Supply Chain Management Concepts

source_concept.label: Describe different types of supply chain management planning.

target_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

relationships.correct_description: The master production schedule is a key output of tactical planning.
relationships.explanation: Tactical planning bridges high-level strategic goals and day-to-day operational execution, and the master production schedule is its primary deliverable. The MPS translates aggregate demand plans into specific production quantities and timelines for individual products. This connection illustrates how planning hierarchy flows from broad supply chain strategy down to actionable production schedules.

sources.title: Mastering the Three Levels of Supply Chain Management
Supply Chain Planning 101: From Basics to Best Practices
Supply Chain Management Concepts

target_concept.label: Describe different types of supply chain management planning.

relationships.correct_description: Static and dynamic are planning approaches used within the broader types of SCM planning.
relationships.explanation: Static and dynamic planning represent execution methodologies that operate within the strategic, tactical, and operational planning framework. A static approach provides a stable reference for operational decisions, while a dynamic approach enables real-time responsiveness. Analyzing how these approaches map to different planning levels reveals their complementary roles in supply chain management.

sources.title: Set up master plans for the deprecated planning engine
Static vs. Dynamic Supply Chains: The Path to Resilience
Static Route vs Dynamic Route Planning: A Practical Guide

target_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

relationships.correct_description: The master production schedule can be managed using either static or dynamic planning methods.
relationships.explanation: A static MPS is regenerated periodically and provides a fixed baseline for procurement and production, while a dynamic MPS updates continuously with each new order or demand change. The choice between static and dynamic approaches affects how quickly the production schedule responds to market shifts. Analyzing this tradeoff is essential for balancing schedule stability against responsiveness in production planning.

sources.title: Set up master plans for the deprecated planning engine
Static vs. Dynamic Supply Chains: The Path to Resilience
Static Route vs Dynamic Route Planning: A Practical Guide

source_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

target_concept.label: Describe different types of supply chain management planning.

relationships.correct_description: The MPS is a key output of tactical-level supply chain planning.
relationships.explanation: The master production schedule sits at the tactical level of the planning hierarchy, translating high-level strategic decisions into specific production commitments. It serves as the critical link between aggregate demand plans and detailed operational execution. Understanding this positioning helps analysts determine how changes at the strategic level cascade into production schedule adjustments.

sources.title: Master production schedule
Master Production Schedule (MPS) Guide
Master Production Schedule (MPS) define in APICS Dictionary 11 Edition

source_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

relationships.correct_description: The MPS uses the bill of materials to determine component and material requirements.
relationships.explanation: When the MPS specifies production quantities for finished goods, the BOM is used to explode those quantities into detailed lists of required components, sub-assemblies, and raw materials. This explosion process is fundamental to material requirements planning, translating top-level production decisions into actionable procurement and manufacturing orders. The accuracy of both the MPS and BOM directly determines whether the right materials arrive at the right time.

sources.title: Master production schedule
Master Production Schedule (MPS) Guide
Master Production Schedule (MPS) define in APICS Dictionary 11 Edition

source_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

target_concept.label: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: The MPS drives raw material procurement through material requirements planning.
relationships.explanation: The master production schedule initiates the material requirements planning process, which calculates the timing and quantities of raw materials that must be procured to support planned production. Without an accurate MPS, raw material orders may be placed too early, tying up capital, or too late, causing production delays. This relationship highlights how production planning directly governs the procurement function in supply chain management.

sources.title: Master production schedule
Master Production Schedule (MPS) Guide
Master Production Schedule (MPS) define in APICS Dictionary 11 Edition

target_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

relationships.correct_description: The BOM is used to explode the MPS into detailed material requirements.
relationships.explanation: The bill of materials provides the structural recipe that allows the MPS to be decomposed into specific component and material needs at each level of the product hierarchy. This BOM explosion process is the core mechanism of MRP systems, converting high-level production plans into granular purchasing and manufacturing instructions. Errors in the BOM cascade through the entire planning process, making BOM accuracy critical to production success.

sources.title: Bills of Material (BOM) In the Supply Chain
What Is a Bill of Materials (BOM)? The Complete Guide to BOMs
Bill of Materials (BOM) in Supply Chain Management: Impacts and Examples

target_concept.label: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: The BOM specifies the raw materials needed for production.
relationships.explanation: The bill of materials provides a complete specification of every raw material required to produce a finished product, including exact quantities and units of measure. This structured list enables procurement teams to determine what to order, when, and in what amounts based on planned production volumes. Analyzing the BOM-to-raw-materials relationship reveals how product design decisions directly influence procurement strategy and supplier requirements.

sources.title: Bills of Material (BOM) In the Supply Chain
What Is a Bill of Materials (BOM)? The Complete Guide to BOMs
Bill of Materials (BOM) in Supply Chain Management: Impacts and Examples

target_concept.label: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: Sub-assemblies listed in a BOM become work-in-process during manufacturing.
relationships.explanation: A multi-level BOM defines sub-assemblies that must be manufactured before final assembly, and these sub-assemblies exist as work-in-process inventory while they are being produced. The BOM structure directly determines how many WIP stages a product passes through and the complexity of the production flow. Analyzing this relationship helps identify potential bottlenecks where WIP accumulation may occur based on the product's structural complexity.

sources.title: Bills of Material (BOM) In the Supply Chain
What Is a Bill of Materials (BOM)? The Complete Guide to BOMs
Bill of Materials (BOM) in Supply Chain Management: Impacts and Examples

source_concept.label: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: The BOM specifies which raw materials are required and in what quantities.
relationships.explanation: Raw materials procurement decisions are governed by the bill of materials, which defines the exact types and quantities of materials needed for each product. Without an accurate BOM, procurement teams cannot determine correct order quantities, leading to either shortages or excess inventory. This dependency illustrates how product engineering documentation directly drives supply chain purchasing activities.

sources.title: What Are Raw Materials?
Raw Materials Inventory: Explanation, Benefits, and Tips
Raw Material - Definition, Importance, Types & Example

source_concept.label: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.

target_concept.label: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: Raw materials become work-in-process once they enter the production process.
relationships.explanation: The transition from raw materials to work-in-process marks the point where inventory changes classification on the balance sheet and begins accumulating labor and overhead costs. This transformation is a critical juncture in the production cycle because once materials enter WIP, they are committed to a specific product and are harder to repurpose. Analyzing this flow helps identify where value is added and where bottlenecks may form in the manufacturing process.

sources.title: What Are Raw Materials?
Raw Materials Inventory: Explanation, Benefits, and Tips
Raw Material - Definition, Importance, Types & Example

source_concept.label: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.

target_concept.label: Define finished goods and the goal to move those goods.

relationships.correct_description: Raw materials are transformed through production into finished goods.
relationships.explanation: The complete production pipeline transforms raw materials through various manufacturing stages into finished goods ready for customer delivery. This end-to-end transformation represents the core value-creation process in manufacturing supply chains. Analyzing the full journey from raw materials to finished goods reveals total lead time, cost accumulation, and the multiple points where quality and efficiency can be improved.

sources.title: What Are Raw Materials?
Raw Materials Inventory: Explanation, Benefits, and Tips
Raw Material - Definition, Importance, Types & Example

source_concept.label: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.

target_concept.label: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: Raw materials become work-in-process once they enter manufacturing.
relationships.explanation: Work-in-process inventory originates from raw materials that have been issued to the production floor and begun the manufacturing process. The rate at which raw materials convert to WIP determines production throughput and influences reorder timing for new materials. Analyzing this upstream dependency helps production planners coordinate material availability with manufacturing schedules to prevent idle production lines.

sources.title: Work in process
The Monthly Metric: Work-in-Process Inventory
What is Work in Process?

source_concept.label: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.

target_concept.label: Define finished goods and the goal to move those goods.

relationships.correct_description: Work-in-process becomes finished goods upon completion of manufacturing.
relationships.explanation: The transition from WIP to finished goods occurs when products complete all manufacturing steps and pass quality inspection, at which point they are reclassified as finished goods inventory available for sale. High WIP-to-finished-goods conversion rates indicate efficient production, while slow conversions signal bottlenecks. Analyzing this transition point is key to optimizing production cycle times and ensuring timely order fulfillment.

sources.title: Work in process
The Monthly Metric: Work-in-Process Inventory
What is Work in Process?

source_concept.label: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.

target_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

relationships.correct_description: The master production schedule drives the flow of WIP through the production process.
relationships.explanation: The MPS determines what products are manufactured and when, which directly controls the volume and timing of work-in-process on the production floor. High MPS variability can lead to WIP buildup at bottleneck operations, while a well-leveled MPS promotes smooth WIP flow. Analyzing how the MPS influences WIP levels helps planners identify scheduling improvements that reduce lead times and carrying costs.

sources.title: Work in process
The Monthly Metric: Work-in-Process Inventory
What is Work in Process?

source_concept.label: Define finished goods and the goal to move those goods.

target_concept.label: Describe work-in-process for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: Work-in-process becomes finished goods upon completion of manufacturing.
relationships.explanation: Finished goods inventory is the direct output of the WIP pipeline, and the rate of WIP completion determines how quickly finished goods become available for customer orders. When finished goods demand increases, production must accelerate WIP throughput to maintain service levels. Analyzing the WIP-to-finished-goods flow reveals whether production capacity aligns with market demand.

sources.title: Finished Goods Inventory: What It Is, Formula, and Examples
Finished Goods Inventory Management: A Guide + Formula
Types of Inventory: Raw, WIP, Finished Goods Explained

source_concept.label: Define finished goods and the goal to move those goods.

target_concept.label: Describe raw materials required for supply chain management.

relationships.correct_description: Finished goods are the end result of transforming raw materials through production.
relationships.explanation: Finished goods represent the culmination of the entire production process that begins with raw material procurement. The cost of finished goods includes all raw material costs plus labor and overhead accumulated during manufacturing. Analyzing this end-to-end relationship helps supply chain managers understand total cost of goods manufactured and identify opportunities for cost reduction from sourcing through final production.

sources.title: Finished Goods Inventory: What It Is, Formula, and Examples
Finished Goods Inventory Management: A Guide + Formula
Types of Inventory: Raw, WIP, Finished Goods Explained

source_concept.label: Define finished goods and the goal to move those goods.

target_concept.label: Explain how a master production schedule is determined.

relationships.correct_description: The master production schedule determines what finished goods will be produced and when.
relationships.explanation: The MPS is the authoritative plan that dictates which finished goods are manufactured, in what quantities, and on what timeline, directly controlling finished goods availability. Finished goods inventory targets and customer demand feed back into MPS adjustments during each planning cycle. Analyzing this bidirectional relationship reveals how demand-side pressures shape production planning and how production decisions affect order fulfillment capability.
